preamble: The diphthong /au/, typically a back and rounded vowel sound, has undergone a notable transformation to /ei/, a front and unrounded diphthong, in the historical development of Faroese. This sound change is characterized by a series of phonetic adjustments that resulted in a significant shift in vowel quality.
phoneticEffects: This transformation involves several key phonetic effects: fronting, where the back vowel elements of /au/ move towards the front of the mouth; raising, as the initial open vowel component of /au/ shifts to a mid-front position; and crucially, delabialization (unrounding), where the rounded back vowel element loses its lip rounding and fronts to an unrounded high-front vowel

examples.note: This shift is attested in the historical phonology of Faroese, where the diphthong originating from Old Norse *au developed into Modern Faroese /ei/. Specific word examples were not explicitly provided in the consulted sources, but the general sound change is documented.
